location.origin: Columbus, Georgia (born August 1, 1953, while father was stationed at Fort Benning). Military family -- Army brat upbringing across Virginia, California, and Germany before settling in Tacoma, Washington, in 1968.
location.formative: Tacoma, Washington (1968-1974). Attended Lakes High School in Lakewood, WA. Met bassist Richard Cousins in 1969; they studied the blues together while playing Tacoma bars. Saw Albert Collins perform at a 1971 graduation party -- Collins told him to 'keep it up.' The formative period is definitively Tacoma: the record collection, the bar circuit, the Collins encounter, the Cousins partnership all happened here.
location.career: Eugene, Oregon (1974-late 1970s): formed the Robert Cray Band with Richard Cousins and Tom Murphy. Collaborated with Curtis Salgado in the Cray-Hawks. Appeared as uncredited bass player in National Lampoon's Animal House (1978). Recorded debut Who's Been Talkin' (Tomato, 1980; label bankrupt). Signed to Hightone Records: Bad Influence (1983), False Accusations (1985). Recorded Showdown! with Albert Collins and Johnny Copeland (Alligator, 1985; Grammy). Signed to Mercury Records: Strong Persuader (1986; 3x Platinum, No. 13 Billboard 200, 'Smoking Gun' No. 1 Rock Tracks, Grammy). Continued Mercury recordings through the 1990s. Five Grammy Awards total. Blues Hall of Fame inductee. Fender signature Stratocaster (1990). Continued recording through 2020s (That's What I Heard, 2020). Active touring into 2025-2026 ('Out Driving Around' tour). Over 50 years as a working musician.

labels: Tomato Records (1980, debut; label went bankrupt)
Hightone Records (1983-1985; Bad Influence, False Accusations)
Alligator Records (1985; Showdown! with Collins and Copeland)
Mercury Records (1986-1997; Strong Persuader through Sweet Potato Pie; primary major-label home)
Rykodisc (1999-2001; Take Your Shoes Off, Shoulda Been Home)

members.name: Robert Cray
members.note: Bandleader, primary songwriter, Fender Stratocaster signature artist

members.name: Richard Cousins
members.note: Co-founder; met Cray in Tacoma 1969; partnership lasted over 20 years

members.name: Tom Murphy
members.note: Original Robert Cray Band drummer in Eugene

members.name: Curtis Salgado
members.note: Portland-area blues musician; sang on Who's Been Talkin'; co-led Cray-Hawks; Belushi connection

associated_musicians.name: Albert Collins
associated_musicians.note: Played Cray's high school graduation party in Tacoma (1971). Told him to 'keep it up.' Later toured together, recorded Showdown! (1985, Grammy). Collins died 1993.

associated_musicians.name: Johnny Copeland
associated_musicians.note: Texas blues guitarist. The three-guitar album won a Grammy for Best Traditional Blues Recording.

associated_musicians.name: Eric Clapton
associated_musicians.note: Cray opened for Clapton on major tours in the late 1980s. Part of the blues-guitar continuum: Clapton (British blues-rock), Vaughan (Texas blues-rock), Cray (PNW R&B-blues).

associated_musicians.name: Stevie Ray Vaughan
associated_musicians.note: The two guitarists are the twin pillars of the 1980s blues revival. Complementary approaches: Vaughan (overdriven, physical), Cray (clean, precise). Both Stratocaster players.

associated_musicians.name: B.B. King
associated_musicians.role: Blues elder, peer in later career
associated_musicians.note: Cray performed alongside King at various events. King represented the tradition that Cray revived.

associated_musicians.name: John Belushi
associated_musicians.note: Visited Eugene during Animal House filming (1977). Saw Salgado/Cray perform. The experience helped inspire The Blues Brothers.

associated_musicians.name: Bruce Bromberg
associated_musicians.note: Co-produced Cray's albums from Who's Been Talkin' through Strong Persuader and beyond. Essential to the Cray sound.

associated_musicians.name: Dennis Walker
associated_musicians.note: Co-produced with Bromberg. Co-wrote material with Cray.

associated_musicians.name: Steve Jordan
associated_musicians.note: Produced Take Your Shoes Off (Grammy-winning, 1999) and Robert Cray & Hi Rhythm (Grammy-nominated, 2017).

theory_nodes.concept: The Deep Root (thirty-sixth artist-city pattern -- the artist raised in a PNW city during formative years who stays regional during the amateur-to-professional transition, builds career on the Pacific Northwest circuit, then achieves national/global recognition while the root city claims the artist as its own; differs from The Export because the Deep Root never leaves the regional soil during formation -- Tacoma to Eugene is a PNW-internal move, not an export; the root stays planted, the branches reach outward)
examples: Robert Cray (degree 35) -- raised in Tacoma (1968-1974), formed band in Eugene, Oregon (1974), toured PNW bar circuit for a decade before Strong Persuader (1986) broke nationally. The root (PNW) was never left during the formative period. Tacoma claims him as its own.

Contrast with The Export (degree 0, Quincy Jones) -- Jones left Seattle entirely for a career in New York, then Los Angeles, then globally. The Export severs the geographic connection during the transition to professional life. The Deep Root maintains it.

Contrast with Ray Charles (degree 30) -- Charles left Seattle for Los Angeles in 1950, three years after arriving. His Seattle period was formative but brief. Cray's PNW period was formative AND professional -- the first decade of the Robert Cray Band was a PNW bar-circuit operation.

The graduation-party encounter with Albert Collins in Tacoma (1971) -- the formative moment that defined Cray's career happened in the root city. The Deep Root's most important musical event occurred at home, not elsewhere.

The Animal House connection -- Cray played in a Hollywood film while living in Eugene, Oregon. The PNW attracted national cultural production (the film was shot at University of Oregon), not the other way around. The root city drew the world to it.
theory_nodes.description: The Deep Root is the thirty-sixth artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes an artist whose formative years in a PNW city lead to a professional career that stays within the Pacific Northwest during the critical amateur-to-professional transition, building a regional reputation on the bar circuit before achieving national or global recognition. The Deep Root differs from The Export (degree 0) because the artist does not leave the region to find success -- the success eventually comes to the artist. Cray moved from Tacoma to Eugene, a 280-mile intra-PNW relocation, not an export to New York or Los Angeles. The Robert Cray Band spent a decade on the PNW bar circuit before Mercury Records signed them. The root stayed planted; the canopy grew outward. The Deep Root is the artist-city pattern of the Pacific Northwest lifer who went global without leaving home first.

ancestors: The Export (degree 0, Quincy Jones) -- the artist who leaves the PNW entirely for a national career. The Deep Root is the structural complement: the artist who stays regional, then goes global.
The Shadow Name (degree 34, The Tempos) -- a hidden entity. The Deep Root is the opposite: a visible, well-documented artist whose regional roots are clear and claimed.

The Incubator (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- Charles was incubated in Seattle's CD, then left. Cray was incubated in Tacoma, moved to Eugene, then broke out. The incubation period is longer and the departure is regional, not national.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-six artist-city patterns across thirty-six degrees. The Deep Root completes a trio with The Export and The Incubator: Export = leave the PNW for national career. Incubator = brief PNW formative period, then leave. Deep Root = extended PNW formative AND professional period, then break through nationally while maintaining regional identity. Three modes of PNW-to-national trajectory.

theory_nodes.concept: The stinging clean (thirty-sixth acoustic element -- the specific timbral quality of a Fender Stratocaster played through a clean amplifier with pickups lowered nearly flush to the pickguard, producing a bright, articulate, twangy tone where every note has a sharp leading edge and clear decay; the acoustic signature of precision and restraint, where dynamic range and fingertip touch matter more than gain, distortion, or volume; the tonal palette that defined the 1980s blues revival and offered a third path between Chicago overdrive and Hendrix psychedelia)
examples: Robert Cray (degree 35) -- 'Smoking Gun' opening riff: clean Stratocaster, pickups lowered to pickguard level, every note placed with precision. The tone is bright, articulate, with a distinctive twang. No distortion, no compression, no fuzz.

Contrast with Stevie Ray Vaughan -- Vaughan's Stratocaster tone was warm, beefy, and overdriven through cranked tube amps with heavy-gauge strings. Cray's tone is the structural opposite: bright, clean, and articulate. Same guitar, opposite philosophy.

The Fender signature Robert Cray Stratocaster (1990) -- three vintage-style single-coil pickups, pickups lowered to pickguard level. 'That's where a lot of Cray's tone comes from -- his fingers, and the real low pickups.' The design codifies the stinging clean.

Contrast with Chicago blues overdrive (Muddy Waters through a cranked Fender Deluxe) -- the Chicago sound uses the amplifier's natural breakup as a timbral element. The stinging clean rejects breakup entirely, trusting the fingers and the song.
The ensemble blend (degree 34) described the composite sound of multiple instruments. The stinging clean is the individual voice that cuts through any ensemble with clarity, not volume.
theory_nodes.description: The stinging clean is the thirty-sixth acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the specific timbral quality of a Fender Stratocaster played through a clean amplifier with the pickups lowered almost flush to the pickguard -- a modification that reduces magnetic pull on the strings, lowers output, widens dynamic range, and makes the guitarist's fingers the primary tonal instrument. The result is bright, articulate, and precise, with a sharp leading edge on every note and a clear, uncompressed decay. The stinging clean defined the 1980s blues revival's tonal palette by offering a third path between the overdriven Chicago blues sound (cranked amps, natural breakup) and the psychedelic blues-rock sound (Marshall stacks, fuzz pedals). Robert Cray proved that blues guitar could be powerful without being loud, expressive without being distorted, and commercially viable without sacrificing the instrument's natural voice. The stinging clean follows the ensemble blend (degree 34) in the engine's acoustic progression: after the composite group sound, the individual voice returns with unprecedented clarity.

ancestors: The ensemble blend (degree 34, The Tempos) -- the collective group sound. The stinging clean returns to the individual instrument with maximum clarity, cutting through the ensemble rather than merging into it.
The room tone (degree 33, Larry English) -- the ambient venue environment. The stinging clean is what the room hears when a single clean Stratocaster speaks.
The groove lock (degree 32, Luther Rabb) -- the bass as immovable foundation. The stinging clean is the guitar voice that rides on top of the groove lock, articulating melody over rhythm.

Guitar blue note (degree 1, Bill Frisell) -- the engine's first guitar-specific acoustic element. The stinging clean is the most detailed guitar-tone element yet: not just the note, but the timbral envelope of the instrument itself.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-six acoustic elements across thirty-six degrees. The stinging clean is the engine's first element defined by a specific instrument configuration (Stratocaster + clean amp + lowered pickups) rather than a performance technique or a room characteristic. It marks the acoustic thread's expansion from what musicians do to what their instruments are -- the intersection of lutherie, electronics, and touch that produces a signature sound.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- the articulate sting (thirty-fifth deployment; the blue note played on a clean-toned Stratocaster where every micro-tonal increment of the bend is audible because distortion does not compress or smear the pitch transition; the departure from the diatonic pitch, the passage through the blue-note zone, the arrival or deliberate stopping short of resolution -- all exposed under a magnifying glass of tonal clarity; the blue note stripped of its electrical armor, where the guitarist's fingers are the only variable)
examples: Robert Cray (degree 35) -- clean Stratocaster bends where the micro-tonal passage from diatonic to blue-note territory is fully audible. No distortion to mask the transition. Every frequency shift exposed.

Contrast with ghost bend (degree 33, Larry English) -- the unrecorded, individual blue note lost to time. The articulate sting is maximally recorded, maximally audible. Ghost bend = maximum absence; articulate sting = maximum presence.

Contrast with unison bend (degree 34, The Tempos) -- the collective blue note played by multiple musicians. The articulate sting is intensely individual: one guitarist, one clean note, one perfectly calibrated bend.

Contrast with Stevie Ray Vaughan's overdriven bends -- Vaughan's distortion compresses the dynamic range and adds harmonic overtones to every bend, creating a thick, sustained pitch shift. Cray's clean bends expose the raw pitch change without harmonic additives.

The 'Smoking Gun' solo -- Cray's bends in the song's guitar solo are textbook articulate stings: clean tone, precise pitch targeting, every micro-tonal moment audible. The solo works because of what is NOT there (distortion, compression, effects) as much as what IS there (touch, dynamics, precision).
theory_nodes.description: Thirty-fifth deployment of the blue note thread. The articulate sting is the blue note played on a clean-toned Stratocaster where the absence of distortion exposes every micro-tonal increment of the pitch bend. On an overdriven guitar, the clipping circuit compresses dynamics and adds harmonic overtones that smear the pitch transition -- the bend sounds thick and sustained but the fine detail is obscured. On a clean guitar with lowered pickups, the bend is transparent: the departure from the diatonic pitch, the passage through the blue-note zone (the quarter-tone and third-tone territory that defines the 'blue' quality), and the arrival at the target or the deliberate stopping short of resolution -- all audible, all exposed. The articulate sting is the blue note under a magnifying glass. It returns the thread to solo performance (after the collective unison bend at degree 34) at the highest level of tonal clarity in the engine's thirty-five deployments.

ancestors: Blue note -- all thirty-four prior deployments (degrees 0-34)
Blue note -- unison bend (degree 34, The Tempos): the collective blue note. The articulate sting returns to the individual voice but at higher clarity than any previous solo deployment.
Blue note -- ghost bend (degree 33, Larry English): the unrecorded solo blue note. The articulate sting is the recorded, maximally audible solo blue note -- structural opposite of the ghost.

Blue note -- boogaloo guitar bend (degree 29, Ivan Jones): the previous guitar-specific blue note. The articulate sting extends the guitar thread with a clean-tone focus that the boogaloo bend's funk context did not require.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-five deployments across thirty-six degrees. The articulate sting completes a three-degree arc: ghost bend (33, absent individual) -> unison bend (34, present collective) -> articulate sting (35, maximally present individual). The blue note thread oscillates between absence and presence, individual and collective, and now reaches its highest-clarity individual deployment.

theory_nodes.concept: Breakthrough tidal (fourth E=5 mode -- the energy of an artist who breaks through from local/regional to national/global recognition; not contained within the local scene like scene-cumulative tidal or collective tidal, not distributed across enterprises like personal tidal; breakthrough tidal is E=5 at escape velocity, where the energy of the local formation period catalyzes into commercial and critical mass at the national level; the energy of Strong Persuader going 3x Platinum, 'Smoking Gun' hitting No. 1, a Tacoma kid standing on stage with Clapton and Collins as a peer)
examples: Robert Cray (degree 35) -- E=5 breakthrough tidal. A decade on the PNW bar circuit built the energy that Strong Persuader released nationally. The Tacoma formation period was the compression; the Mercury Records breakthrough was the detonation.

Luther Rabb (degree 32) -- E=5 personal tidal. Career-cumulative energy across multiple enterprises (Velvetones, Ballin' Jack, Santana, War). Energy distributed across a long career, not concentrated in a single breakthrough moment.
Larry English (degree 33) -- E=5 scene-cumulative tidal. Individual within a high-energy ecosystem. The scene generated the energy; the individual embodied it.
The Tempos (degree 34) -- E=5 collective tidal. Group generating energy as a unit. The band format amplifies E=5 through multiple performers.

The four-degree E=5 streak (32-35) now maps four distinct modes from individual (32) through scene (33) through group (34) to breakthrough (35). The progression traces energy from contained to escape velocity.
theory_nodes.description: Breakthrough tidal is the fourth E=5 mode in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the energy of an artist whose local formation period builds sufficient creative and professional momentum to catalyze a national or global breakthrough. The energy is not merely high (E=5) but directional -- it breaks outward from the regional to the national, from the bar circuit to the Billboard charts, from the PNW to the world. Robert Cray's breakthrough tidal is the energy of a decade of Pacific Northwest bar-circuit woodshedding compressed into the moment when Strong Persuader went triple-platinum and 'Smoking Gun' reached No. 1. The E=5 distribution after degree 35 is 2:13:6:11:4 (E=1:E=2:E=3:E=4:E=5). Four consecutive E=5 degrees with four distinct modes represents the most sustained high-energy passage in the engine.

ancestors: Personal tidal (degree 32, Luther Rabb) -- individual career energy. Breakthrough tidal extends personal energy to the moment of national recognition.
Scene-cumulative tidal (degree 33, Larry English) -- scene-level energy. Breakthrough tidal is what happens when scene energy propels an individual beyond the scene's geographic boundaries.
Collective tidal (degree 34, The Tempos) -- group energy. Breakthrough tidal can apply to groups (if a group breaks through nationally) but here describes a bandleader's individual trajectory.
genealogy.relationship: Four E=5 modes across four consecutive degrees. The progression personal (32) -> scene (33) -> collective (34) -> breakthrough (35) traces energy from individual to local ecosystem to group to global escape velocity. The four modes may not be exhaustive -- future E=5 degrees may reveal additional modes -- but they establish E=5 as a multi-dimensional category, not a single state.

seattle_connection: TACOMA -- first city outside Seattle proper. Robert Cray raised in Tacoma (1968-1974), attended Lakes High School in Lakewood, met Richard Cousins in Tacoma bars, saw Albert Collins at Tacoma graduation party (1971). THE DEEP ROOT as 36th artist-city pattern -- PNW lifer who stayed regional during formation (Tacoma to Eugene) then broke through nationally. THE STINGING CLEAN as 36th acoustic element -- clean Stratocaster tone with lowered pickups, the sound of precision and restraint. THE ARTICULATE STING as 35th blue note deployment -- clean-tone bend where every micro-tonal increment is audible. E=5 FOURTH CONSECUTIVE (degrees 32-33-34-35) -- four modes: personal tidal (32), scene-cumulative tidal (33), collective tidal (34), breakthrough tidal (35). Distribution 2:13:6:11:4. BLUES AT GENRE STAGE 17 -- new genre, root genre mapped. First time Blues appears in the engine. MERCURY RECORDS x3 (degrees 31, 32, 35). SOLO FORMAT returns after group (34). CENTRAL DISTRICT STREAK BREAKS at degree 35 -- first non-CD degree after three consecutive CD degrees (32-33-34). DOCUMENTATION ABUNDANCE -- after two degrees of gaps (33-34), degree 35 completes the documentation spectrum: absence (33), misdirection (34), abundance (35). GENDER: 6:30 ratio (~16.7%). Cray is male. No change in women count.

theory_genealogy: The Deep Root (thirty-sixth artist-city pattern; PNW lifer who stays regional during formation then breaks through nationally; Tacoma to Eugene to global; root stays planted, branches reach outward)
The stinging clean (thirty-sixth acoustic element; clean Stratocaster with lowered pickups; bright, articulate, precise; the tonal palette of the 1980s blues revival; restraint as power)

Blue note -- the articulate sting (thirty-fifth deployment; clean-tone bend where every micro-tonal increment is audible; the blue note under a magnifying glass; structural opposite of the ghost bend)
Breakthrough tidal (fourth E=5 mode; energy at escape velocity; local formation catalyzes into national/global breakthrough)
engine_sequencing: E=5 FOURTH CONSECUTIVE: Degrees 32-33-34-35. Distribution 2:13:6:11:4. Four modes: personal tidal (32), scene-cumulative tidal (33), collective tidal (34), breakthrough tidal (35). Track whether E=5 continues at degree 36.
GENRE Stage 17 at degree 35: Blues (NEW). Root genre mapped. Track at degree 36.
STINGING CLEAN opens clean-tone acoustic phase: precision over power. Track element 37 at degree 36.
MERCURY RECORDS x3 (degrees 31, 32, 35). Most frequent major label. Track at degree 36.
TACOMA as second city: engine expands from Seattle to Puget Sound. Track geography at degree 36.
CENTRAL DISTRICT STREAK BROKEN: Three consecutive (32-33-34), broken at 35 (Tacoma). Track whether CD returns at degree 36.
SOLO FORMAT returns: Cray after The Tempos (34). Track format at degree 36.
DOCUMENTATION SPECTRUM COMPLETE: absence (33), misdirection (34), abundance (35). Engine has methodology for all three states.
All engine sequencing items from degrees 0-34
cultural_patterns: Artist-city taxonomy: THIRTY-SIX named patterns (adding The Deep Root at degree 35). Classify every degree.
TACOMA NODE: 1 degree (35). First non-Seattle node. Track for recurrence.
CENTRAL DISTRICT CLUSTER: 8 degrees (0, 3, 4, 5, 30, 32, 33, 34). Streak broken at 35. Track.
PNW-TO-NATIONAL PIPELINE: Jones (0), Charles (30), Cray (35) = three artists who went from PNW to global. Three different modes: Export, Incubator, Deep Root.
E=5 STREAK: 4 consecutive (32-33-34-35). Four modes. The longest maximum-energy run. Track for continuation.
BLUES AS ROOT GENRE: The engine has been mapping branches (R&B, Soul, Jazz) for 34 degrees. At 35, the trunk is mapped.
1980s BLUES REVIVAL: Cray and Vaughan as twin pillars. The engine documents one of the two.
All cultural pattern items from degrees 0-34
